summary: Jeff Atwood criticizes Radio Shack's website for displaying raw ASP errors to users, using it as a jumping-off point to advocate for ASP.NET adoption. He takes issue with Joel Spolsky's argument that porting from classic ASP to .NET offers no ROI, calling it blind to the 'massive, obvious benefits' of the newer framework. While acknowledging he's not a fan of ASP.NET's 'winforms on the web' abstraction model, Atwood argues the framework represents an order of magnitude improvement over classic ASP, particularly for error handling.
key_points: Production websites from major corporations shouldn't expose raw error messages to users
ASP.NET provides dramatically better error handling capabilities than classic ASP
Joel Spolsky's 'no ROI' argument against porting to .NET ignores obvious technical benefits
Despite its flaws, ASP.NET is an order of magnitude improvement over classic ASP
Sometimes framework upgrades are worth the investment even when they don't directly add customer-facing features
money_quotes: If I was running a giant corporation, I think I'd hire coders who could develop a rational error handling strategy for our production website.
I'm not a big fan of the ultra-leaky 'winforms on the web' abstraction model, but there's no denying that ASP.NET – and the .NET framework – is an order of magnitude improvement over ASP.
It's difficult to comprehend how a smart guy like Joel could be so blind to the massive, obvious benefits of a straight port to ASP.NET.
Coherent global error handling is only the tip of the iceberg.
At this point, you could not pay me enough to write in ASP.
Ok, you could. But it would be really expensive.
key_insight: Technical debt and outdated frameworks have real costs that go beyond feature development, and dismissing framework upgrades as 'no ROI' ignores the cumulative benefits of modern tooling.
